focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The photograph was small enough to miss if you wanted to. It sat halfway down a tabloid site between a footballer's divorce and a television chef's tax trouble, four grainy frames under a headline about a married patroness slipping east after midnight. Nina's face was mostly turned away. Jonas Hale's corrugated studio door showed clearer than she did. The Ashdown Mercedes took up the front of the shot, black paint holding a stripe of streetlight and enough of the plate to make it theirs.

By half past nine it had already moved through more hands than Beni liked to imagine. One of the florists on the front railings had it open on her phone while dragging damp cardboard trays from the van. The porter in the mews had folded a printout in quarters and slid it under a clipboard as if hiding it turned it back into paper. In the basement corridor somebody had sent it to the house printer, grayscale and cheap, and the hot-toner smell sat over stale coffee, wet wool, and the van exhaust slipping in whenever the service door opened.

Beni parked by the garage wall and killed the engine. The jacket he had lifted off Eszter's chair a few hours earlier still held Nina's cash in the hidden pocket. The mechanical hush rushed in after the fan died, leather cooling under him, his own breath too loud for a second. On the passenger seat lay the route wallet for the day, clean enough from the outside. Inside it were timings, pick-up slips, the ordinary work of getting people rich enough never to know what their own days cost. One blurred photograph on a gossip site had turned all that ordinary work sharp.

He took the wallet upstairs by the service stair and found Marian Bell waiting outside Oscar's morning room with one shoe off.

Marian worked from Oscar's City office three...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...stove. The child's voice started again in a burst of Hungarian complaint, bright enough to make the kitchen feel briefly full of a life that had nothing to do with him or Nina or the garage. Eszter listened with her head bent, one hand braced on the counter, and he knew from the shape of her shoulders that the conversation was over unless he was ready to break something open properly.

He wasn't ready.

He took the envelope from the table, then the dress bag and the pharmacy packet. At the door he looked back once. Eszter had already set a pot on the hob and was reaching for the onions. She had already stepped into the next task and left him outside the circle of it.

Upstairs the house was warmer and quieter, the heat from the radiators trapped in carpets thick enough to swallow footfall. He climbed with Nina's dress bag knocking his leg and the envelope pressing against his ribs, and by the second landing he was already arranging the next version of the day in his head. Nina had kept him late after the gallery. Nina had paid extra because of the hours. Anything beyond that had to stay blurred at the edges. Sunday was uncertain. Sunday was probably work. The car smelled of spray because he had been overcareful. Later, if Eszter still let him try again, he would have to come up with something better than that.

Each lie was light enough on its own. That was the ugly part. The work was in keeping them from touching, in remembering which stair belonged to which life, which woman got the clean answer and which one got the useful one. By the time he reached Nina's door he could already feel the strain in it the way he felt bad alignment through the wheel, before thought, before judgment. The car would still go where you pointed it. It would just keep asking for correction.
